title: Daniel and Revelation: One Prophetic Story
summary: Daniel gives Revelation much of its prophetic vocabulary: kingdoms, beasts, judgment, sanctuary, time periods, and final deliverance.

sections.title: A shared prophetic world
body: Daniel and Revelation are not two unrelated prophetic books. Revelation constantly assumes the world Daniel has already opened. Daniel introduces successive kingdoms, symbolic beasts, arrogant powers, heavenly judgment, sanctuary cleansing, and final deliverance. Revelation takes those themes and carries them forward through the history of the church toward the return of Christ.

This is especially important because Revelation often alludes rather than explains. When John sees a beast from the sea with features of leopard, bear, and lion, Daniel 7 is not optional background. When Revelation speaks of time periods, persecution, blasphemy, and judgment, Daniel's prophecies supply the grammar. Revelation expands Daniel, but it does not abandon him.

sections.title: Kingdom and judgment
body: Daniel 2 shows a sequence of kingdoms ending with the kingdom God establishes. Daniel 7 repeats the sequence with beasts and then lifts the reader into the heavenly court, where the Ancient of days sits, books are opened, and the Son of man receives dominion. Revelation begins with the same conviction: earthly empires are real, but their authority is temporary and answerable to heaven.

Revelation's throne scenes should therefore be read as more than devotional pictures. They are judicial and political in the highest sense. The throne judges all thrones. The Lamb takes the scroll because He alone is worthy to carry history toward its appointed end. The saints suffer under earthly powers, but the final verdict belongs to God.

sections.title: Sanctuary and prophetic time
body: Daniel's sanctuary prophecy does not disappear in Revelation. Daniel 8 points to a conflict that reaches the sanctuary and announces a period after which the sanctuary is vindicated. Daniel 9 returns to Jerusalem, sacrifice, Messiah, covenant, and the prophetic timetable that anchors the longer vision. Revelation continues that sanctuary concern by repeatedly showing Christ's heavenly ministry, the altar of incense, the ark, the temple, and judgment-hour proclamation.

The point is not date-setting as an end in itself. Prophetic time serves the larger sanctuary story. God is not absent from history. Christ's ministry is not vague. The gospel is preached in relation to judgment, worship, and final accountability. Revelation 14's announcement that the hour of God's judgment has come stands in the same biblical world as Daniel's visions.

Daniel also teaches the reader to expect symbolic time to carry historical weight. The time, times, and half a time of Daniel 7 stands beside Revelation's forty-two months and 1,260 days. Together they point to a long period in which a blasphemous power opposes truth and persecutes the saints. The A.D. 538-1798 span is not an arbitrary insertion; it fits the prophetic pattern of a long religious-political dominance followed by a decisive wound.

Daniel 8-9 also prepares the reader for the judgment-hour setting that becomes prominent in Revelation. The sanctuary, the cleansing/vindication, the Messiah, and the prophetic timetable converge in a way that explains why 1844 becomes a major turning point in the reading of Revelation 10 and 14. Revelation 10 shows the little book opened, prophetic time ending, sweetness turning bitter, and a renewed mission. Revelation 14 then announces the judgment hour to the world.

sections.title: Final deliverance
body: Daniel ends with Michael standing, a time of trouble, deliverance of those written in the book, resurrection, and the shining of the wise. Revelation brings that hope to its fullness. The Lamb's people overcome. Babylon falls. The dead are judged. Death and the grave are ended. God dwells with His people, and the curse is removed.

The two books therefore move together from conflict to judgment to restoration. They do not offer escape from history but hope within history. They teach the church to endure because the final word is not beast, Babylon, persecution, or death. The final word belongs to the kingdom of God and the Lamb.

This shared story also protects Revelation from being read as a detached end-time code. Daniel begins in the prophet's own world, follows successive powers, opens the heavenly court, and ends in deliverance. Revelation begins in John's world, follows the church through conflict and warning, opens the heavenly sanctuary, and ends in the New Jerusalem. The pattern is continuous, historical, sanctuary-centered, and deeply pastoral.

Reading Daniel and Revelation together keeps prophecy biblical. Daniel protects Revelation from being detached from history, and Revelation shows Daniel's prophecies moving through Christ, the church, final worship, judgment, and restoration.
The result is not a colder reading but a steadier one. The same God who ruled over Babylon, Medo-Persia, Greece, and Rome still governs history from the throne.
